exemplar.title: The World in Black and White

detailedExemplarData.title: The World in Black and White

strengths: Engaged interdisciplinary communication by consulting the biology teacher
Applied robust research skills using primary/secondary sources and CAARDS evaluation
Collected and analyzed peer feedback on design options to inform refinements
Developed moodboards and concept boards demonstrating creative thinking
Demonstrated disciplined self-management through detailed to-do lists and photoshoot planning
Prepared clear oral presentation notes to convey objectives effectively
Incorporated visual examples (e.g., color-vision diagrams) to support information synthesis
weaknesses: Research questions are clear but not explicitly linked to specific product content decisions
Communication-skill examples lack concrete instances of product revisions made based on feedback
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The student illustrates strong ATL skill application, notably in research, self-management, communication, and creative thinking. Interdisciplinary collaboration and data-driven design choices underpin the projectâs development. To elevate this, they should explicitly trace how inquiry shaped album content and cite precise adjustments made from feedback to demonstrate clear impact on product quality.

strengths: Clear articulation of the global context situating the project
Presence of a structured success-criteria table identifying key elements
An outlined action plan capturing major project phases and tasks
weaknesses: Learning goal lacks specific, measurable success criteria and milestones
Success-criteria table omits quantifiable indicators for each element
Action plan misses detailed sequencing, resource allocation, and explicit timelines
No personal anecdotes provided to deepen connection to the learning goal
Evaluation methods are not clearly aligned to assess each success criterion
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: While the report effectively establishes its global context and outlines core tasks and criteria, the planning framework requires more precision. The learning goal and success criteria need quantifiable targets and deadlines. Resource lists, sequencing steps with dates, and personal reflections would enhance feasibility and ensure direct alignment between evaluation tools and criteria.

strengths: Quantitative survey results (84.8% photo-quality, 100% cover impact) provide solid evidence of success
Reflective narrative on overcoming time-management challenges through to-do lists
Thoughtful linkage of the project to global inclusivity and IB learner profiles
Insightful examination of initial misconceptions about color blindness showing conceptual growth
Self-assessment of developing inquiring and open-minded skills with personal examples
weaknesses: Analysis of peer feedback lacks depth on specific editing or selection improvements
Future learning reflections are not explicitly mapped back to the defined success criteria
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The student offers a comprehensive reflection, blending quantitative data with qualitative insights that highlight personal and academic growth. They connect outcomes to broader IB attributes and demonstrate evolving subject understanding. Strengthening the evaluation would involve deeper dissection of peer feedback to guide future revisions and clearer mapping of how ongoing skill development relates to predetermined success benchmarks.

annotations.comment: Engaging your biology teacher and detailing that interaction showcases strong communication skills and interdisciplinary collaboration.
Collecting and analysing peer feedback on layout options demonstrates adaptability and data-driven decision-making in refining your product.
You outline your research questions clearly, but include specific examples of how this inquiry directly informed your productâs content to deepen evidence of skill application.
Describing how you structured tasks and used to-do lists presents a strong self-discipline narrative, showing strategies to overcome time-management challenges.
Your global context section effectively situates your project but would benefit from personal anecdotes to illustrate a convincing connection to your interests.
The extended action plan outlines tasks but would be stronger with sequencing and resource allocation. Include specific dates, tools, and support contacts for each activity.
While your product goal describes the album concept, the plan would benefit from outlining resources, sequencing, and a timeline to ensure feasibility of production phases.
Excellent description of your research skill applicationâprimary and secondary sources, CAARDS evaluationâdemonstrates effective evidence-based information gathering.

Your learning goal articulates desired skills but lacks specific, measurable success criteria. Consider defining clear milestones (e.g., mastering editing techniques by set dates) to assess progress effectively.
The success criteria table identifies key elements but lacks measurable indicators for each level. Add quantifiable targets (e.g., number of hero shots, colour-palette consistency metrics).
The action plan captures phases but misses detailed task descriptions, resources, and intermediate deadlines. Expand each phase with clear steps and supporting materials.
Connecting your project to global inclusivity emphasizes thoughtful reflection on the wider impact and alignment with IB learner profile attributes.
Survey results on photo quality (84.8% positive) provide quantitative evidence of your productâs success against quality criteria.
Incorporating your oral presentation notes highlights proactive preparation and clarity in conveying project objectives to diverse audiences.
When discussing communication skills applied to your product, give concrete examples of revisions made to the album based on feedback to demonstrate impact on quality.
Your plannerâs To-Do lists exhibit disciplined self-management, effectively balancing personal project tasks with academic responsibilities.
Moodboard development and annotated concept board reflect creative thinking and ensure thematic coherence for your album-cover design.
You effectively challenge your initial misconceptions about colour blindness, illustrating clear growth in your understanding and confidence in the topic.
Your reflection on developing inquiring and open-minded skills links personal growth to the project, showing meaningful evaluation of learning outcomes.
100% positive response to the coverâs visual impact compellingly demonstrates your productâs ability to capture attention and meet success criteria.
You identify photo quality improvements but deepening your analysis of peer feedback would clarify specific editing or selection enhancements for future projects.
Including visual examples from your research excerpts (e.g., color vision diagrams) strengthens evidence of ATL skill application in synthesising complex information.
Your handwritten photoshoot plan outlines clear locations, moods, and objectives, demonstrating proactive self-management in executing your product goal.
While you articulate future learning impact, explicitly linking these skills back to specific success criteria would strengthen your evaluation of their ongoing relevance.
Your evaluation section outlines methods but lacks clear alignment between evaluation tools, survey questions, and your success criteriaâensure each tool assesses specific criteria directly.
